era_context: The late 15th century is characterized by the consolidation of European nation-states, the height of the Italian Renaissance, the Ottoman Empire's expansion under Mehmed II, the Ming Dynasty's stable rule in China, and the acceleration of European exploration. In Iberia, the Catholic Monarchs are consolidating power; in the Americas, the Aztec and Inca empires remain dominant regional powers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond Portuguese coastal contacts
Interior Southeast Asia and the Pacific Islands

events.title: Death of Charles the Bold and End of Burgundian Independence
events.region: Duchy of Burgundy, Low Countries, France
events.description: Charles the Bold, Duke of Burgundy, died on 5 January 1477 at the Battle of Nancy, marking the end of Burgundian independence as a major European power. Charles had attempted to expand Burgundian territory and establish it as an independent kingdom, but his military ambitions were thwarted by the French king Louis XI and Swiss allies. His death without a male heir led to the fragmentation of Burgundian territories, with the French crown annexing much of Burgundy proper while the Low Countries (including the wealthy provinces of Flanders and Brabant) passed to Charles's daughter Mary of Burgundy through her marriage to Maximilian of Habsburg. The death of Charles the Bold marked a turning point in European politics, ending the brief period of Burgundian power and establishing the foundation for the rise of the Habsburg dynasty.
key_figures: Charles the Bold (Duke of Burgundy, d. 1477)
Maximilian of Habsburg (Mary's husband, future Holy Roman Emperor)
Louis XI of France (rival of Charles the Bold)

sources.name: Burgundian chronicles and records; French royal chronicles

events.certainty_note: The death of Charles the Bold on 5 January 1477 at the Battle of Nancy is well-documented in Burgundian, French, and other European sources.
cross_references: 1477-002 (Marriage of Mary of Burgundy and Maximilian)

events.title: Marriage of Mary of Burgundy and Maximilian of Habsburg
events.region: Duchy of Burgundy, Low Countries, Holy Roman Empire
events.description: Following the death of Charles the Bold in January 1477, his daughter Mary of Burgundy married Maximilian of Habsburg in August 1477. This marriage united the wealthy Low Countries with the Habsburg dynasty, establishing the foundation for Habsburg dominance in Europe for centuries to come. The marriage was politically significant because it prevented the French king Louis XI from annexing all of Burgundian territories and established the Habsburgs as a major European power. The union of Burgundy and the Habsburgs would eventually lead to the creation of the Spanish Empire under Charles V and the Austrian Empire under the Habsburgs.
key_figures: Mary of Burgundy (Duchess of Burgundy, r. 1477–1482)
Maximilian of Habsburg (future Holy Roman Emperor, r. 1493–1519)
Louis XI of France (rival of the Habsburgs)

sources.name: Burgundian chronicles and records; Habsburg family records

events.certainty_note: The marriage of Mary of Burgundy and Maximilian of Habsburg in August 1477 is well-documented in Burgundian and Habsburg sources.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration: Continued Expansion Along West African Coast
events.description: Portuguese exploration and trading post expansion continued along the West African coast in 1477 under the direction of King Afonso V. Portuguese merchants and explorers were establishing trading posts and forts to facilitate the gold trade and other commercial activities. The Portuguese were gradually pushing further south along the African coast, seeking to circumnavigate Africa and reach the Indian Ocean. By 1477, the Portuguese had established a network of trading posts from the Senegal River to the Congo River.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and merchant records

events.certainty_note: Portuguese expansion in 1477 is documented in Portuguese royal records, though specific details of individual trading posts in this year are sometimes unclear.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Consolidation Under Emperor Chenghua
events.description: Emperor Chenghua of the Ming Dynasty continued his consolidation of imperial authority in 1477. His reign (1464–1487) was marked by relative peace and stability, though it also saw the rise of eunuch power at court. The emperor's reign saw continued economic development and the consolidation of Ming control over China and its tributary states.

sources.name: Ming imperial records and court chronicles

events.certainty_note: Emperor Chenghua's reign and consolidation of power are well-documented in Ming imperial records.

events.title: Inca Empire: Topa Inca Yupanqui's Expansion and Administrative Reforms
events.description: Topa Inca Yupanqui continued his expansion and consolidation of the Inca Empire in 1477. His military campaigns extended Inca control over vast territories, and his administrative reforms reorganized the empire into a more centralized and efficient system. The construction of roads, administrative centers, and agricultural terraces continued under his direction.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers); Spanish colonial chronicles

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is uncertain; dates are reconstructed from Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions. Topa Inca's reign is traditionally dated 1471–1493.

events.title: Aztec Empire: Axayacatl's Continued Rule and Military Campaigns
events.description: Axayacatl, the sixth Aztec emperor, continued his rule in 1477. His military campaigns and administrative reforms had consolidated Aztec power, and the empire was at the height of its territorial extent. The Aztec state was characterized by a complex system of tribute collection, religious ceremonies, and military organization that maintained control over a vast territory and diverse population.

sources.name: Aztec codices (Codex Mendoza, Codex Florentinus); Spanish colonial chronicles

events.certainty_note: Aztec chronology is reconstructed from post-conquest Spanish sources and Aztec codices. Axayacatl's reign is traditionally dated 1469–1481.

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War: Continuation of Naval Conflict
events.description: The Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) continued in 1477 with ongoing naval conflicts and territorial disputes in the Eastern Mediterranean. Venice, once the dominant naval power in the Mediterranean, was gradually losing ground to the expanding Ottoman Empire under Mehmed II. The war would conclude with the Treaty of Constantinople in 1479, which would mark a significant Ottoman victory and the end of Venetian naval dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles; Venetian diplomatic records and chronicles

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Venetian-Ottoman War in 1477 is well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The death of Charles the Bold is sometimes portrayed as a sudden and dramatic event that immediately ended Burgundian power, but some scholars argue that Burgundian influence continued through the Low Countries and the Habsburg connection. The significance of the marriage of Mary of Burgundy and Maximilian has been debated; some scholars emphasize it as a turning point in European history, while others view it as one of many dynastic marriages of the period. The dating of Charles the Bold's death is sometimes given as 1476 rather than 1477, though 5 January 1477 at the Battle of Nancy is the most commonly accepted date.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1477 is rich for Europe, the Ottoman Empire, and East Asia, but sparse for sub-Saharan Africa beyond Portuguese coastal contacts and the Americas. European sources are dominated by court chronicles and diplomatic records. The death of Charles the Bold and the marriage of Mary of Burgundy and Maximilian are extensively documented in Burgundian, French, and Habsburg sources, reflecting the significance of these events to European politics. Aztec and Inca history is reconstructed from post-conquest Spanish sources and oral traditions, with significant dating uncertainties.

graph_edges.note: Death of Charles the Bold led to marriage of Mary of Burgundy and Maximilian
Marriage of Mary of Burgundy and Maximilian led to Maximilian's rise to power
Venetian-Ottoman War led to Treaty of Constantinople in 1479
